title: Last state execution for male sodomy in continental Europe
description: The Batavian Commonwealth (in present-day Netherlands) carries out continental Europe's last recorded state-sanctioned execution of a man for same-sex sodomy.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Charity Bryant and Sylvia Drake begin their partnership in Vermont
description: Vermont residents Charity Bryant and Sylvia Drake enter a lifelong relationship, one of the earliest well-documented same-sex couples in United States history, later chronicled by historian Rachel Hope Cleves.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Duchy of Warsaw re-legalizes same-sex relations
description: With the creation of the Duchy of Warsaw, French-modelled law comes into force on Polish territory, making same-sex sexual activity legal again.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Netherlands decriminalizes homosexual conduct
description: The Netherlands abolishes its laws against homosexual conduct, ending criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kingdom of Bavaria decriminalizes consensual same-sex acts
description: The Kingdom of Bavaria repeals its laws against homosexual conduct between consenting adults, one of the earliest German states to do so.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Term "crime against nature" enters a US criminal code
description: The phrase "crime against nature" appears for the first time in a criminal code in the United States, establishing wording later used to prosecute same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Same-sex relations re-criminalized in Prussian-annexed Poland
description: After the Duchy of Warsaw is partitioned between Prussia and the Russian Empire, Prussian law re-criminalizes same-sex sexual activity in the territory Prussia annexed.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dominican Republic decriminalizes homosexuality
description: The Dominican Republic removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: El Salvador decriminalizes homosexuality
description: El Salvador removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Attack on the Marquis de Custine forces him into the open
description: The Marquis de Custine is beaten and left for dead in Saint-Denis after propositioning a soldier. The resulting scandal exposes his sexuality publicly; he goes on to live openly with his partner Edward St. Barbe while keeping an active social life in Paris.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Empire of Brazil decriminalizes homosexuality
description: The Empire of Brazil removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts with its new criminal code.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Russian Empire criminalizes sex between men under Article 995
description: The Russian Empire outlaws muzhelozhstvo, interpreted by courts as anal sex between men, under Article 995 of its criminal code. Convicted men lost their legal rights and faced four to five years of exile in Siberia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bolivia decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Bolivia removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Homosexuality criminalized in Congress Poland
description: Congress Poland, the part of Poland under Russian rule after the Partitions, makes homosexuality illegal for the first time in the region's history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: James Pratt and John Smith hanged in Britain's last execution for homosexuality
description: James Pratt and John Smith are hanged at Newgate prison in London after being caught together in private lodgings — the last known execution for homosexuality in Great Britain.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Kingdom of Hannover decriminalizes consensual same-sex acts
description: The Kingdom of Hannover repeals its laws against homosexual conduct between consenting adults.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Birth of Leona Florentino, mother of Philippine lesbian literature
description: Poet Leona Florentino is born in the Spanish-colonized Philippines. Her writing later challenges the colonial patriarchy and lays the foundation of lesbian literature in the archipelago.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Portugal decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Portugal removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Argentina decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Argentina removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Earliest known reference to lesbians in Mormon records
description: A Salt Lake City man's diary notes a Mormon woman "trying to seduce a young girl" — the first known reference to lesbians in Mormon history.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ottoman Empire decriminalizes homosexuality
description: During its Tanzimat reform period, the Ottoman Empire — predecessor of modern Turkey — removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: England ends the death penalty for "buggery"
description: The Offences against the Person Act 1861 removes the death sentence for "buggery" in England, a punishment last carried out in 1836. The penalty becomes imprisonment of 10 years to life.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: San Marino decriminalizes homosexuality
description: San Marino removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs defends homosexuality before the Congress of German Jurists
description: In Munich, Karl Heinrich Ulrichs pleads before the Congress of German Jurists for a resolution to repeal anti-homosexual laws and is shouted down — the earliest known public self-outing in defence of homosexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Word "homosexual" appears in print for the first time
description: An anonymous German pamphlet by Austrian-born novelist Karl-Maria Kertbeny, arguing against a Prussian anti-sodomy law, contains the earliest known printed use of the word "homosexual".

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Suriname decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Suriname removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Joseph and His Friend, possibly the first American gay novel, is published
description: Bayard Taylor's Joseph and His Friend: A Story of Pennsylvania appears in print, regarded by some as the first American novel centered on a homosexual relationship.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Paragraph 175 criminalizes male homosexuality across the German Empire
description: The Reich Criminal Code's Paragraph 175 makes sexual acts between males a crime throughout the newly unified German Empire. The Nazis later broadened the provision in 1935, and thousands of the men prosecuted under it died in concentration camps; the paragraph remained on the books until 10 March 1994.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Guatemala decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Guatemala removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mexico decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Mexico removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Empire of Japan decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: The Empire of Japan repeals its ban on anal sodomy between men, a prohibition that had only been introduced in the early Meiji Restoration years.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Paraguay decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Paraguay removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Labouchere Amendment outlaws "gross indecency" between men in the UK
description: Queen Victoria gives royal assent to the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1885, whose Clause 11 — the Labouchere Amendment — criminalizes sexual acts between men beyond sodomy, while sex between women goes unmentioned. The popular tale that Victoria struck out references to lesbianism is a legend; women were simply never in the bill.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: We'wha of the Zuni visits Washington and meets President Cleveland
description: We'wha, a lhamana (two-spirit) member of the Zuni tribe, begins a six-month stay in Washington, D.C., during which they call on President Grover Cleveland.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Portugal re-criminalizes homosexual acts
description: Portugal reinstates criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts, reversing its 1852 decriminalization.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Cleveland Street Scandal breaks in London
description: A police investigation into a male brothel on Cleveland Street in London sets off a public scandal implicating aristocratic clients, fuelling prosecution and public hostility toward men who had sex with men in Victorian Britain.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Italy legalizes homosexuality through the Zanardelli Code
description: The new Codice Zanardelli takes effect in Italy, removing consensual same-sex acts from the reach of the criminal law nationwide.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Homosexuality legalized on the territory of the Vatican
description: With the Zanardelli Code in force, same-sex acts become legal on the territory of the Vatican, which at the time fell under Italian jurisdiction.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: "Bisexual" and "heterosexual" enter English in their modern senses
description: Charles Gilbert Chaddock's English translation of Krafft-Ebing's Psychopathia Sexualis introduces the words "bisexual" and "heterosexual" in the meanings they carry today.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Birth of bisexual poet Edna St. Vincent Millay
description: Edna St. Vincent Millay, the popular American poet who lived openly as bisexual, is born.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Birth of sexuality researcher Alfred Kinsey
description: Alfred Kinsey, the biologist whose later studies reshaped scientific understanding of human sexuality, is born.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Oscar Wilde convicted of "gross indecency"
description: Oscar Wilde is prosecuted under the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1885 for "gross indecency" and sentenced to two years of hard labor, a conviction that destroyed his career and became a lasting symbol of Victorian persecution.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Adolfo Caminha publishes Bom-Crioulo in Brazil
description: Adolfo Caminha's controversial novel Bom-Crioulo (The Black Man and the Cabin Boy) appears in Brazil, placing homosexuality at the center of its story and casting a Black man as its hero.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Magnus Hirschfeld petitions the Reichstag against Paragraph 175
description: Magnus Hirschfeld submits a petition to the Reichstag calling for the abolition of Paragraph 175, opening a repeal campaign he would pursue for the rest of his life.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Order of Chaeronea, England's first homosexual rights group, is formed
description: George Cecil Ives organizes the Order of Chaeronea, the earliest known homosexual rights group in England.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Honduras decriminalizes homosexuality
description: Honduras removes criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts.

sources.label: Wikipedia — LGBTQ rights in the 19th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
